title: The Seven Petitions
html:
Big Question

What are we really asking for in each of the seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer?

Tradition tells us that Saint Augustine, now elderly, was preaching in Hippo one Sunday when he stopped his sermon and asked the congregation: "How many of you have prayed the Our Father today?" Nearly every hand went up. Then he asked again: "And how many of you know what you are asking for in each word?" Many hands came down. Augustine smiled and said: "Do not worry. Today we are going to open this prayer like someone opening a treasure chest. There are seven jewels inside. Seven petitions that Christ himself chose for us. Three launch us toward God; four bring us his answer. And all together they are the pathway to heaven." In his letter to Proba, Augustine wrote that "whatever words we use, we say nothing other than what is contained in the Lord's Prayer." Because the Our Father is not one prayer among many: it is the total prayer. In seven petitions, Jesus sums up everything a human being needs and everything God wants to give us.

The Three Petitions Directed to God: "Thy"

The Catechism divides the seven petitions into two groups. The first three carry us toward God: his Name, his Kingdom, his Will. In the Latin original, Saint Matthew uses the pronoun "thy" three times (CCC 2803). We do not begin by asking for ourselves; we begin by desiring the glory of the Father. It is a gesture of love: before my bread, your glory. Before my problem, your Kingdom.

Activity

Pray the Our Father very slowly. Pause after each petition and ask yourself: Do I truly desire this? Do I say it with my heart or from memory? Mark the petition you find hardest to truly feel.

First Petition: "Hallowed Be Thy Name"

"Hallowed be thy name" (CCC 2807). In the Bible, the "name" of someone is not just a label; it is the person himself, his very identity. To hallow God's name means to acknowledge it as holy, to treat it with reverence, and above all: to live in such a way that others, seeing us, glorify God. "Hallowed be thy name" is not asking God to become holier —for he already is— but asking that his holiness be recognized in us and through us. It is a petition for coherence: that our lives not be a mockery of the name we bear as Christians.

The Catechism explains: "The term 'to hallow' is to be understood here not primarily in its causative sense (only God hallows, makes holy), but above all in an evaluative sense: to recognize as holy, to treat as holy" (CCC 2807). When a Christian steals, lies, or is unfaithful, God's name is blasphemed among non-believers (Rom 2:24). When a Christian loves, forgives, and serves, God's name is hallowed. Each of us can make God's name loved or despised, depending on how we live.

Connection

Think of people you know who do not believe in God. What image of God are they receiving by watching you? Does your life make God's name more lovable or harder to believe? Be honest with yourself.

Second Petition: "Thy Kingdom Come"

"Thy Kingdom come" (CCC 2816). In the Old Testament, the people waited for a king who would liberate Israel. But Jesus did not come to establish a political kingdom, but the Kingdom of God: a kingdom of truth and life, of holiness and grace, of justice, love, and peace (Preface of Christ the King). To ask that the Kingdom come is to ask that Christ reign in our hearts, in our families, in our society.

The Kingdom of God "is already present" (LG 3), but has not yet reached its fullness. It grows silently like a seed (Mk 4:26-29), like yeast in the dough (Mt 13:33). When we love our neighbor, when we work for justice, when we pray, we are making the Kingdom present. But above all, "the Kingdom of God is Christ himself" (Origen). To say "thy Kingdom come" is to say "Come, Lord Jesus" (Rev 22:20). This petition is also a cry of hope: Maranatha, come, Lord! The world as it is —with wars, hunger, injustice— is not God's final plan. We ask that the Kingdom come because we believe that history has a glorious destiny (CCC 2818).

Third Petition: "Thy Will Be Done on Earth as It Is in Heaven"

"Thy will be done" (CCC 2822). This is perhaps the most difficult petition of the Our Father. Because asking that God's will be done means renouncing our own when they come into conflict. It is not bitter resignation, but filial trust. In heaven, the angels and saints fulfill God's will with instant and perfect joy; we ask that the same happen on earth.

My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me; yet not as I will, but as you will.

Matthew 26:39

Jesus prayed this petition in Gethsemane with sweat of blood, and fulfilled it all the way to the cross. Doing the Father's will was his food (Jn 4:34). For us, doing God's will is not obeying a capricious tyrant, but collaborating with a Father who loves us and whose plan is our true happiness. "The world and its desires pass away, but whoever does the will of God remains forever" (1 Jn 2:17). This petition unites us to Christ's "yes" and to Mary's "fiat" (Lk 1:38). It is the petition of trusting love, even when we do not understand (CCC 2824-2826).

The Four Petitions for Our Needs: "Us"

After the three petitions centered on God, the Our Father turns toward us with four petitions that encompass our whole life: sustenance, forgiveness, protection, and liberation. "Give us," "forgive us," "lead us not," "deliver us." Four verbs that express our radical poverty and our trust in the Father.

Fourth Petition: "Give Us This Day Our Daily Bread"

"Give us this day our daily bread" (CCC 2828). This petition stands at the very center of the Lord's Prayer. We ask for bread: the most basic, the most humble thing. Bread is the symbol of everything we need to live: food, shelter, work, health. But by saying "our bread," we do not say "my bread": bread is for everyone. No one can pray this petition from the heart while hoarding as a brother or sister goes hungry. "Daily bread" is also the bread of the Eucharist. The Church Fathers saw in this petition a direct reference to the Bread of Life. "We do not only ask for material bread —says Saint Cyprian— but also for Christ, who is the living bread come down from heaven." We ask for the bread of the Word and the Bread of the Sacrament. And we ask for "this day": we do not worry about tomorrow (Mt 6:34), but trust in the Father's daily providence (CCC 2836-2837).

Context

In the Jewish tradition, God fed Israel with manna in the desert, but only allowed them to gather enough for one day (Ex 16:4). Whoever gathered more found their manna rotten the next morning. The lesson is clear: trust in God each day, without anxiously hoarding. The fourth petition of the Lord's Prayer takes up exactly that attitude of daily trust.

Fifth Petition: "Forgive Us Our Trespasses as We Forgive Those Who Trespass against Us"

"Forgive us our trespasses as we forgive those who trespass against us" (CCC 2838). This is the only petition Jesus explicitly commented on after teaching the Our Father: "If you forgive others their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you. But if you do not forgive others, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses" (Mt 6:14-15). It is a terrible and wonderful condition. Terrible because it makes our forgiveness depend on the forgiveness we give. Wonderful because it gives us the power to open the floodgates of God's mercy (CCC 2841).

The Catechism is blunt: "This petition is so important that it is the only one to which the Lord returns and develops in the Sermon on the Mount. The forgiveness of our trespasses is linked to the forgiveness we grant" (CCC 2840). Why this severity? Because the one who does not forgive closes himself off from grace. It is like keeping your fist clenched: you cannot receive anything until you open it. Forgiving is not easy, especially when the wound is deep. But it is possible with the grace of God. We do not forgive with our own strength; we forgive with the forgiveness we first receive from Christ.

Sixth Petition: "Lead Us Not into Temptation"

"Lead us not into temptation" (CCC 2846). This petition can sound strange. Does God tempt us? The letter of James is clear: "God tempts no one" (Jas 1:13). What we ask is something else: we ask for the strength not to succumb to the test. We ask for discernment to distinguish between the trial that purifies and the temptation that leads to sin. We ask that God not permit us to take the path that leads to sin (CCC 2846-2847).

The Holy Spirit helps us discern: trial is necessary for growth, like an athlete training; temptation is the invitation to evil, which we must reject. Jesus himself was tempted in the desert and taught us to resist with the Word of God. In Gethsemane he exhorted the disciples: "Watch and pray so that you will not fall into temptation" (Mt 26:41). This petition reminds us of our weakness: alone we cannot overcome. We need grace. "Spiritual combat is inseparable from the combat of prayer" (CCC 2849).

Going Deeper

The precise translation has been debated. In 2019, Pope Francis suggested that the translation "lead us not into temptation" could give the mistaken impression that God pushes us into temptation. Some episcopal conferences (such as the French and Italian) have already changed to "do not let us succumb to temptation" or "do not abandon us in temptation." The Catechism clarifies: we ask God not to allow us to take the path that leads to sin, and to give us the strength of the Holy Spirit to resist (CCC 2848-2849).

Seventh Petition: "Deliver Us from Evil"

"Deliver us from evil" (CCC 2850). This is the final petition, and it closes the Our Father with a cry of victory. We do not ask to be delivered from an abstract evil, but from the Evil One —from the devil— who opposes God's plan. In Jesus' prayer at the Last Supper, he asked the Father not to take us out of the world, but to guard us from the Evil One (Jn 17:15). This seventh petition takes up that plea.

I am not asking you to take them out of the world, but I ask you to guard them from the Evil One.

John 17:15

The evil from which we ask to be delivered is not primarily physical evils —sickness, poverty, accidents—, though we also ask for protection against them. The evil from which we ask to be delivered is sin, eternal death, separation from God. It is the final victory of Christ over Satan. "The Church asks in this final petition that God the Father deliver us from the power of Satan and grant us peace" (CCC 2854). This petition sums up everything we are asking: that at the end of our lives and of history, evil will be defeated and God will be all in all (1 Cor 15:28).

The entire prayer of the Lord's Prayer prepares us for this final battle. The seven petitions form a path: from God's glory (first three) to our need (last four), from the hallowing of the Name to deliverance from the Evil One. Like a ladder that climbs to heaven and descends to earth, the Our Father takes us by the hand and leads us from our clay to the heart of the Father. "If we go through every petition of this prayer —says Saint Augustine— we will find nothing that is not contained in them."

Biblical Connection: A Prayer That Embraces Everything

Pray then in this way: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come. Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our trespasses, as we also have forgiven those who trespass against us. And do not bring us to the time of trial, but deliver us from the evil one.

Matthew 6:9-13

Saint Paul, without explicitly citing the Our Father, seems to have it in mind when he writes to the Ephesians: "That you may have power, together with all the Lord's holy people, to grasp how wide and long and high and deep is the love of Christ, and to know this love that surpasses knowledge" (Eph 3:18-19). The seven petitions have that breadth and depth: they embrace heaven and earth, soul and body, time and eternity. No dimension of human existence falls outside this prayer.

That is why the saints have so loved the Our Father. Saint Thérèse of Lisieux said that sometimes, when her soul was so dry she could not formulate a single prayer of her own, she would pray just one Our Father very slowly, savoring each word. "It is the prayer that lifts me highest —she wrote—, because it is the one Jesus himself composed." Saint Teresa of Avila recommended lingering at length on each petition, especially on "thy will be done" and "forgive us our trespasses." And Blessed Carlo Acutis, the young saint of the 21st century, said: "When you pray the Our Father, say 'yes' to each petition with all your heart. It is the best way to prepare for heaven."

For Reflection

  1. Of the seven petitions, which one do you pray with the most awareness? Which one do you say most mechanically without thinking?
  2. Is there someone you have still not forgiven? The fifth petition binds you. What step can you take this week?
  3. Do you live anxiously about the future (money, health, work) or do you trust like the manna, day by day?
  4. How does your life hallow God's name? Are there areas where you are dishonoring it?
  5. When you pray "thy will be done," do you say it with a child's trust or with fear?

Key Idea

Key Idea: The seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer are the complete map of the Christian life: three lead us to desire God's glory, four open us to receive his daily mercy. In them everything is contained.

Cultural Resources

Saint Augustine and the Letter to Proba — Around the year 412, a Roman widow named Proba wrote to Saint Augustine asking him how she should pray. The bishop of Hippo's reply is one of the most beautiful catecheses on the Lord's Prayer. Augustine tells her she does not need many words, because "whatever we say with words, if it belongs to true prayer, we will find nothing that is not already contained in that prayer of the Lord." He recommends going through the seven petitions one by one, lingering on each according to the need of the day. This letter, known as Epistle 130, is essential reading for understanding Christian prayer.

James Tissot: "The Lord's Prayer" in Art — The French painter James Tissot (1836-1902), after a profound religious conversion, devoted the last years of his life to painting scenes from the life of Jesus. His series on the Lord's Prayer, known as "Le Pater Noster," includes detailed illustrations for each of the seven petitions, showing how they are lived out in daily life. Images of workers praying together, families sharing bread, and the sick finding peace are a visual catechesis that connects prayer with human reality. These works are housed at the Brooklyn Museum.

Music: "The Lord's Prayer" in Musical Tradition — Composers such as Igor Stravinsky ("Pater Noster," 1926), Giuseppe Verdi (in his "Pezzi sacri"), and Maurice Duruflé ("Notre Père," 1977) have set the Lord's Prayer to music. The Aramaic version —the language Jesus spoke— sung by the Jerusalem community is a striking experience: hearing the prayer in its original words helps one feel its historical closeness. Also worth knowing is the version by Marco Frisina, a contemporary Italian composer, whose "Padre Nostro" has become very popular in parishes worldwide.
